Releases

Every signed binary.

Every release of One Link, signed end-to-end and published with a reproducible-build attestation. The canonical record lives in the public git repository on GitHub. This page is the human-readable index of it.

All releases on GitHub → Get the current build
Current

v0.21.0-alpha.0

The current build is an alpha. Windows + Linux ship signed binaries today. macOS + Android + iOS ship from source while the signing rigs come online. Every platform builds cleanly from source on every release.

  • Coherence Mesh: 10 of 10 rows shipped (Phase 1 of Row 10 confidential-compute scaffold).
  • Sphinx onion routing: T1.1 Ristretto + T1.2 PQ-hybrid blinding + T1.3 field-binding + T1.5 Schnorr aggregation all live.
  • Coherence-field substrate: production with daemon couplings (homology -> field, field -> prefetch, field -> ratchet).
  • 3,565 tests passing across Rust workspace + Python daemon regression suite.

See the site changelog for everything shipped since v0.1.0, and the download page for the current platform-by-platform state.

How releases work

Every binary is signed and reproducible.

Ed25519 release signature.

Every binary is signed offline with an Ed25519 release key. The verifier on the download page checks the signature in your browser against the pinned pubkey before you ever run the bytes.

Reproducible attestation.

Every release ships next to a signed attestation document that proves which source commit produced it and which sealed build environment ran the compiler. Anyone can rebuild from source and verify byte equality.

Post-quantum signatures land v0.22.

Today's release signatures are Ed25519. The ML-DSA-65 half of the hybrid (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65) lands once the offline release rig is provisioned. The crate is shipped and verified today; the operational rig follows.

One Link delivers itself.

The /download/ button does not fetch a file over plain HTTP. It opens a real One Link session with the release relay, negotiates a hybrid key, and streams the binary chunk by chunk through the same protocol your phone will use tomorrow.

Earlier releases

The full history is on GitHub.

Every tag, every signed binary, every attestation document lives in the public release feed alongside the source.

Daemon releases → Website releases

One Link

A free, private network for messages, files, and devices. Owned by no one. Belongs to everyone.
